text: The laparoscopic view shows the excision of an endometriosis nodule located on the rectum. The surgeon carefully manipulates instruments to remove the visible mass from the rectovaginal septum.

Upon entry, the surgeon observes complete obliteration of the posterior cul-de-sac. Filmy adhesions tethering the sigmoid colon to the left pelvic side wall are removed, allowing cranial mobilization of the sigmoid and identification of the ureter at the pelvic brim. Sharp dissection separates the left ovary from the underlying rectum, and the rectum is dissected off the posterior uterus. The left ovary is bluntly mobilized out of the posterior cul-de-sac and temporarily sutured to the round ligament. The right ovary is bluntly mobilized off the pelvic sidewall, and the right ureter is identified transperitoneally. The retroperitoneum is entered below the ureter for complete dissection on the right pelvic sidewall.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to carefully detach rectal attachments from the posterior uterus, revealing multiple attachment points. The first attachment is freed, improving visualization of the rectum and the remaining nodule. Dissection proceeds laterally to release the nodule from sidewall attachments. The nodule is meticulously dissected off the posterior uterus using electrosurgery at the interface. The surgical field is continuously assessed to prevent injury. The mass is further mobilized from the right pelvic sidewall, with a hand in the vagina aiding in distinguishing healthy vaginal tissue from the nodule. The right ovary is temporarily tethered out of the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully removes the peritoneum overlying the right pelvic sidewall, avoiding the ureter. A rectal probe is placed, and the endometriosis nodule is sequentially excised from its lateral attachments and freed from the rectum. Residual nodule tissue is grasped with a tenaculum and dissected using cold scissors, exposing a friable area of the muscularis layer. This area is oversewn in multiple layers with barbed PDO suture, ensuring the rectal lumen is not narrowed, tested by passing an EEA sizer through the rectum. The peritoneum overlying the left pelvic sidewall is also excised, completing the excision, followed by a rectal leak test.
The patient was discharged home post-op day zero, had a routine post-op course, and reported significantly improved pain at her post-op visit. All specimens removed were notable for endometriosis.
